news_summary: Today's human rights progress highlights advancements in gender equality, LGBTQ+ recognition, corporate climate accountability, and global peacebuilding efforts.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés aujourd'hui en matière de droits de l'homme mettent en évidence les avancées en matière d'égalité entre les sexes, de reconnaissance de la communauté LGBTQ+, de responsabilité des entreprises face au changement climatique et d'efforts mondiaux en faveur de la consolidation de la paix.
news_summary_es: Los avances actuales en materia de derechos humanos ponen de relieve los progresos en la igualdad de género, el reconocimiento de la comunidad LGBTQ+, la responsabilidad de las empresas en materia climática y los esfuerzos por fomentar la paz a nivel mundial.

articles.title: Period tax scrapped in Pakistan: what the ruling means for women
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Two lawyers filed a court case. Thousands signed a petition. Pakistan just agreed to scrap its sales tax on period products. Finance Minister Muhammad Aurangzeb announced the move this month, calling sanitary products “daily necessities that are indispensable for women’s health, dignity and full participation in social activities.” The […]
The post Period tax scrapped in Pakistan: what the ruling means for women first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Two lawyers filed a court case. Thousands signed a petition. Pakistan just agreed to scrap its sales tax on period products.

Finance Minister Muhammad Aurangzeb announced the move this month, calling sanitary products “daily necessities that are indispensable for women’s health, dignity and full participation in social activities.” The decision follows a legal challenge filed last year by Mahnoor Omer, 25, and Ahsan Jehangir Khan, 29, who argued the charges were a discriminatory “pink tax” on women. Their petition gathered thousands of signatures and turned into a real public conversation online.

What the taxes cost in practice

Pakistan levies an 18 percent sales tax on locally made period products. Imported items face an additional 25 percent customs charge on top of that. Those rates have pushed commercial menstrual products out of reach for most women in the country. According to UNICEF research, only a minority use them. Most rely on cloth or homemade alternatives, which carry higher infection risks.

The real win may be cultural

Removing the sales tax won’t immediately change much of that. A price drop won’t reach women priced out by income, geography, or simply not having a nearby store. But there’s a different argument for why this matters.

Bushra Mahnoor, executive director of Mahwari Justice, a Pakistani period rights organization, told CNN the most valuable effect of the tax change may be what it says out loud: that menstruation is a health matter, not a private embarrassment.

“Menstrual justice also means access to clean water, sanitation facilities, accurate menstrual education and a society free from period stigma,” she said. “This moment is significant, but our work is far from over.”

UN Women called menstrual health a question of “health, dignity and equality, not a luxury,” and said the change would help women stay employed and girls stay in school.

What comes next

Omer welcomed the announcement and made clear the campaign would keep pushing on the remaining charges, including customs duties on imported products. The sales tax is one piece; she and her co-counsel are already working on the rest.

The government also announced it will scrap the 18 percent sales tax on contraceptives. Aurangzeb linked the move to population growth, calling family planning “a top priority” and noting Pakistan ranks fifth globally by population size.

The tax change is on the books. Getting it to translate into actual access for the women who need it most is the work that’s still ahead.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant policy change in Pakistan scrapping the sales tax on period products, which positively impacts women's health, dignity, and equality. This decision addresses a discriminatory financial barrier affecting a large population and promotes menstrual justice, with broader implications for social inclusion and gender equality. The article provides detailed context about the legal challenge, societal impact, and ongoing efforts, making it a substantial and inspiring story.
articles.personality_title: Pakistan removes sales tax on period products to support women’s health
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Pakistan, women have faced extra taxes on menstrual products, making them expensive and hard to buy. These taxes included an 18 percent sales tax on local products and a 25 percent customs charge on imported items.

**What happened** – This month, Pakistan’s Finance Minister Muhammad Aurangzeb announced the government will remove the sales tax on period products. This decision came after two lawyers, Mahnoor Omer and Ahsan Jehangir Khan, filed a court case and thousands signed a petition arguing the tax was unfair to women.

**Impact** – The tax removal helps make menstrual products more affordable and recognizes menstruation as a health issue, not something to be ashamed of. While prices may not drop immediately for everyone, the change sends an important message against period stigma and supports women’s dignity and participation in society.

**What’s next step** – Activists plan to continue fighting to remove other taxes, like customs charges on imported products. The government also removed the sales tax on contraceptives, linking it to family planning efforts. The challenge now is to ensure these changes help women across Pakistan actually get easier access to these products.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Pakistan has ended the sales tax on period products, marking a key step toward fairer access to menstrual health and reducing stigma for women nationwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Pakistan supprime la taxe sur les produits menstruels pour soutenir la santé des femmes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Au Pakistan, les femmes devaient payer des taxes supplémentaires sur les produits menstruels, ce qui les rendait coûteux et difficiles à acheter. Ces taxes comprenaient une TVA de 18 % sur les produits locaux et une taxe douanière de 25 % sur les produits importés.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Ce mois-ci, le ministre des Finances pakistanais, Muhammad Aurangzeb, a annoncé que le gouvernement allait supprimer la TVA sur les produits menstruels. Cette décision fait suite à une action en justice menée par deux avocats, Mahnoor Omer et Ahsan Jehangir Khan, et à une pétition signée par des milliers de personnes, qui dénonçaient une taxe injuste envers les femmes.

**Impact** – La suppression de la taxe aide à rendre les produits menstruels plus abordables et reconnaît que la menstruation est un problème de santé, et non une honte. Même si les prix ne baisseront pas immédiatement pour toutes, ce changement envoie un message important contre la stigmatisation des règles et soutient la dignité et la participation des femmes dans la société.

**Prochaine étape** – Les militants prévoient de continuer à lutter pour supprimer d’autres taxes, comme les droits de douane sur les produits importés. Le gouvernement a également supprimé la TVA sur les contraceptifs, en lien avec les efforts de planification familiale. Le défi est maintenant de faire en sorte que ces changements facilitent réellement l’accès à ces produits pour toutes les femmes au Pakistan.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Pakistan a supprimé la TVA sur les produits menstruels, une étape importante vers un meilleur accès à la santé menstruelle et la réduction de la stigmatisation pour les femmes dans tout le pays.
articles.personality_title_es: Pakistán elimina el impuesto sobre productos menstruales para apoyar la salud de las mujeres
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Pakistán, las mujeres enfrentaban impuestos adicionales sobre los productos menstruales, lo que los hacía caros y difíciles de comprar. Estos impuestos incluían un impuesto al valor agregado del 18 % sobre los productos locales y un cargo aduanero del 25 % sobre los productos importados.

**Qué pasó** – Este mes, el ministro de Finanzas de Pakistán, Muhammad Aurangzeb, anunció que el gobierno eliminará el impuesto sobre las ventas de productos menstruales. Esta decisión llegó después de que dos abogados, Mahnoor Omer y Ahsan Jehangir Khan, presentaron un caso judicial y miles firmaron una petición argumentando que el impuesto era injusto para las mujeres.

**Impacto** – La eliminación del impuesto ayuda a que los productos menstruales sean más accesibles y reconoce que la menstruación es un asunto de salud, no algo de lo que avergonzarse. Aunque los precios no bajarán de inmediato para todas, este cambio envía un mensaje importante contra el estigma de la menstruación y apoya la dignidad y la participación de las mujeres en la sociedad.

**Próximo paso** – Los activistas planean seguir luchando para eliminar otros impuestos, como los aranceles sobre productos importados. El gobierno también eliminó el impuesto sobre las ventas de anticonceptivos, vinculándolo con los esfuerzos de planificación familiar. El reto ahora es asegurar que estos cambios faciliten realmente el acceso a estos productos para las mujeres en todo Pakistán.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Pakistán eliminó el impuesto sobre las ventas de productos menstruales, un paso clave hacia un acceso más justo a la salud menstrual y la reducción del estigma para las mujeres en todo el país.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a blossoming lotus flower cradling a collection of menstrual pads and contraceptive pills gently nestled within its petals, set against a soft, natural palette of earthy reds, creams, and greens, symbolizing health, dignity, and the breaking of stigma around menstruation and reproductive rights in Pakistan.

articles.title: Denied by the State, demanded by the streets: Japan's LGBTQ+ turning point
articles.summary: Japanese LGBTQ+ communities are taking to the streets to push for equal marriage rights with the slogan: "May love prevail in the Supreme Court."
articles.content:
This post is part of Global Voices’ June 2026 Spotlight series, “Gender Diversity.” This series offers insight into gender diversity and how it is being threatened, protected, and preserved around the world. You can support this coverage by donating here.

The streets of Tokyo have transformed into a vibrant sea of rainbow flags, sharp techno beats, and thousands of chanting voices for Tokyo Rainbow Pride this June. This year’s Pride Month is more than a celebratory festival of visibility; the atmosphere feels electrified as the Japanese Supreme Court is expected to issue its first unified constitutional ruling on same-sex marriage, and LGBTQ+ communities are taking to the streets to push for equal rights to marriage with the slogan: “May love prevail in the Supreme Court” (最高裁で愛が勝つ).

Legal battle: Marriage for all

The Supreme Court ruling will settle constitutional debates among lower district courts over whether the Japanese Civil Code's current exclusion of same-sex marriage violates the Japanese Constitution in early 2027.

Japan’s legal battle for the right to equal marriage began on February 14, 2019, with 13 same-sex couples simultaneously filing lawsuits against the Japanese government in district courts across Tokyo, Osaka, Nagoya, Fukuoka, and Sapporo.

In March 2024, the Sapporo High Court first ruled that the provisions of the Civil Code and the Family Register Act that do not allow same-sex couples to become families are unconstitutional. Four other district high courts hold similar rulings, and the prevailing view is that the current system violates Article 14 (equality under the law), Article 13 (the right to the pursuit of happiness), and Article 24, Section 2 (individual dignity and the essential equality of the sexes) of the Constitution of Japan.

However, on November 28, 2025, a Tokyo High Court ruled against its peers, holding that same-sex couples are not included in the constitutional definition of marriage and that any change to that definition should be the responsibility of Japan’s National Diet.

With the support of the LGBT+ activist group Marriage for All Japan, the plaintiffs have brought their case to the Japanese Supreme Court for appeal. As explained by legal scholar Yasuhiko Watanabe, the Tokyo High Court’s ruling would grant “unchecked discretion to the conservative government.”

The Japanese government's conservative stance

Japan remains the only G7 nation that does not provide comprehensive national legal protections or marriage rights for same-sex couples.

Despite data from both the conservative Yomiuri and the liberal Asahi showing solid majorities in support of same-sex marriage, at 65 percent and 72 percent, respectively, in 2023, Japan’s Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi and the ruling Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) remain unsupportive of same-sex marriage. During her election campaign in September 2025, Takaichi also referred to the definition of marriage to argue against same-sex marriage rights:

私は基本的には同性婚には反対の立場だ。憲法で結婚は両性の同意によるとされている。だから、現時点で同性婚に賛同する立場ではない。

I am fundamentally opposed to same-sex marriage. The Constitution defines marriage as being between a man and a woman. Therefore, at this time I do not support same-sex marriage.

Currently, same-sex couples are excluded from spousal benefits in over 100 laws that involve insurance, pensions, etc. They rely on regional partnership certification systems, which began in Tokyo in 2015 and have since spread to more than 500 regions, that offer same-sex couples limited, non-binding benefits, such as hospital visitation rights.

In response to the legal challenges in recent years, instead of amending the existing marriage law to grant sexual minority equal rights, the conservative government has recognized registered same-sex couples as “de facto marriage status” in a few dozen laws, and passed an LGBTQ+ Understanding Law in 2023 to promote public understanding of sexual minorities and prevent “unfair discrimination.” Only after three years of stalling did the government recently roll out its first national plan to raise awareness and strengthen support systems for the LGBTQ+ community.

However, the recognition of “de facto marriage” through the partnership certification system would institutionalize discrimination, legal scholar Yasuhiko Watanabe argued:

仮に、事実婚制度の整備によって配偶者控除や相続権などの不利益が一定程度解消されるとしても、婚姻と区別された制度にとどまる限り、そこには象徴的な格差が残ります。異性のカップルだけが婚姻の制度に属し、同性カップルはそこから「排除された」別の制度に属するという構図は、社会的に“セカンドクラス”という印象を与えかねません。

Even if the ‘de facto marriage’ system could, to a certain extent, address some disadvantages, such as the spousal tax deduction and inheritance rights, as long as it remains a separate system distinct from marriage, it will institutionalise a symbolic segregation. If only heterosexual couples belong to the marriage system and same-sex couples are ‘excluded’ into a separate system, it risks creating a ‘second-class’ stigma [among the sexual minorities].

Moreover, even with the same-sex partnership registration and “de facto marriage” system, same-sex couples cannot enter into the national household registration system (Koseki), which is structured around a heterosexual norm, with a wife, a husband, and their unmarried offspring under a single surname to track the family lineage. In Japan, rather than the individual, the family is the administrative unit, and the legal status, rights, and welfare of citizenship and parentage are registered through the Koseki system. Being excluded from the household registration system, same-sex couples could not enjoy automatic inheritance, spousal tax deduction, spousal visa, child adoption, etc.

Yet, within the National Diet, conservative lawmakers are resistant to changing the marriage law, fearing it would lead to amending the Koseki system and thereby trigger a domino effect that would rewrite the nationality law and hundreds of civil laws related to government administration.

Can love win?

LGBTQ+ activists considered the Japanese Supreme Court’s monumental decision to bring the legal disputes to the 15-Justice Grand Bench a significant move to break the decade-long political stagnation. If the Grand Bench rules the current marriage law unconstitutional, the court may impose a deadline for the National Diet to rewrite the marriage and Koseki systems, or to establish a civic union registration system with a separate household registration system. The worst scenario is to follow the Tokyo High Court verdict and let the National Diet determine the mechanism for protecting sexual minorities’ equal rights.

Since May 2026, Marriage for All Japan has launched a nationwide “Love Wins” campaign to demonstrate public support for full same-sex marriage rights and vowed to spread the message with the slogan, “May love prevail in the Supreme Court,” across 47 prefectures during Pride Month.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant and ongoing legal and social movement in Japan advocating for equal marriage rights for LGBTQ+ individuals, focusing on the upcoming Supreme Court ruling that could bring constitutional recognition and broad societal benefits. It covers the legal battles, public support, and activism in detail, showing a meaningful push toward equality and human rights in a major G7 country.
articles.personality_title: Japan’s LGBTQ+ community awaits Supreme Court decision on same-sex marriage
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Japan, same-sex marriage is currently not legally recognized. While many local courts have ruled that this exclusion is against the constitution, the government and some courts disagree. The issue is now headed to Japan’s highest court for a final ruling.

**What happened** – Since 2019, several same-sex couples filed lawsuits to challenge Japan’s marriage laws. In 2024, some high courts said banning same-sex marriage is unconstitutional. But in late 2025, Tokyo’s High Court disagreed, saying only lawmakers can change the law. Now, the case is before the Supreme Court, expected to make a decision in early 2027. Meanwhile, thousands of people showed support at Tokyo Rainbow Pride, hoping the court will rule in favor of marriage equality.

**Impact** – This Supreme Court ruling is important because it could change the law nationwide and allow same-sex couples to marry legally for the first time in Japan. Currently, same-sex couples miss out on many benefits like inheritance rights, tax breaks, and family registration. The ruling could also push lawmakers to update Japan’s family registration system, which is based on traditional ideas of marriage.

**What's next step** – The Supreme Court will decide if the current law violates Japan’s constitution. If it rules in favor of same-sex marriage, the government may have to change marriage and family laws. If it rules against, the fight for legal marriage equality will continue through political and social efforts.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Japan’s Supreme Court is set to decide whether same-sex couples can marry legally, a step that could reshape family rights across the country.
articles.personality_title_fr: La communauté LGBTQ+ japonaise attend la décision de la Cour suprême sur le mariage homosexuel
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Au Japon, le mariage entre personnes de même sexe n’est pas reconnu légalement. Plusieurs tribunaux locaux ont jugé cette exclusion contraire à la constitution, mais le gouvernement et certains tribunaux ne sont pas d’accord. L’affaire est maintenant portée devant la plus haute juridiction du pays.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Depuis 2019, plusieurs couples de même sexe ont déposé des recours contre la loi sur le mariage. En 2024, certains tribunaux ont déclaré l’interdiction du mariage homosexuel inconstitutionnelle. Mais fin 2025, la Cour d’appel de Tokyo a estimé que seul le Parlement pouvait changer la loi. L’affaire est maintenant devant la Cour suprême, dont la décision est attendue début 2027. Pendant ce temps, des milliers de personnes ont manifesté leur soutien lors de la Pride de Tokyo.

**Impact** – Cette décision de la Cour suprême est importante car elle pourrait légaliser le mariage pour tous à l’échelle nationale. Actuellement, les couples de même sexe n’ont pas accès à de nombreux droits comme l’héritage, les réductions d’impôts ou l’enregistrement familial. Cette décision pourrait aussi pousser à réviser le système d’enregistrement des familles au Japon, fondé sur des idées traditionnelles du mariage.

**Prochaine étape** – La Cour suprême doit décider si la loi actuelle viole la constitution japonaise. Si la décision est favorable, le gouvernement devra changer les lois sur le mariage et la famille. Sinon, la lutte pour l’égalité des droits continuera par des actions politiques et sociales.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La Cour suprême du Japon va décider si les couples de même sexe peuvent se marier légalement, un pas qui pourrait transformer les droits familiaux dans tout le pays.
articles.personality_title_es: La comunidad LGBTQ+ de Japón espera la decisión del Tribunal Supremo sobre el matrimonio igualitario
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Japón, el matrimonio entre personas del mismo sexo no está reconocido legalmente. Muchos tribunales locales han dicho que esta exclusión viola la constitución, pero el gobierno y algunos tribunales no están de acuerdo. Ahora el caso está en la Corte Suprema para una decisión final.

**Qué pasó** – Desde 2019, varias parejas del mismo sexo presentaron demandas para desafiar las leyes de matrimonio. En 2024, algunos tribunales superiores dijeron que prohibir el matrimonio igualitario es inconstitucional. Pero a fines de 2025, el Tribunal Superior de Tokio dijo que solo el Parlamento puede cambiar la ley. Ahora el caso está ante la Corte Suprema, que decidirá a principios de 2027. Mientras tanto, miles de personas mostraron su apoyo en el Tokyo Rainbow Pride.

**Impacto** – Esta decisión de la Corte Suprema es clave porque podría legalizar el matrimonio igualitario en todo Japón. Actualmente, las parejas del mismo sexo no tienen muchos beneficios como derechos de herencia, deducciones fiscales o registro familiar. La decisión también podría impulsar cambios en el sistema de registro familiar japonés, basado en ideas tradicionales.

**Próximo paso** – La Corte Suprema decidirá si la ley actual viola la constitución japonesa. Si decide a favor, el gobierno tendrá que cambiar las leyes de matrimonio y familia. Si decide en contra, la lucha por la igualdad legal continuará por vías políticas y sociales.

**Resumen en una frase** – La Corte Suprema de Japón decidirá si las parejas del mismo sexo pueden casarse legalmente, un paso que podría cambiar los derechos familiares en todo el país.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a large, diverse crowd of glowing, intertwined rainbow-colored origami cranes soaring upward above Tokyo’s iconic skyline at dusk, symbolizing unity and hope for LGBTQ+ marriage equality, with a subtle, abstract depiction of a balanced judicial scale formed by two gently curved bridges connecting opposite riverbanks beneath them.

articles.title: 'Big polluting companies like Total have climate obligations under French law'
articles.summary: Genie Godula is pleased to welcome Justine Ripoll, Campaign Manager for French NGO "Notre Affaire à Tous", and one of four NGOs who brought the climate case against Total Energy. She argues that climate litigation not as a symbolic confrontation with the fossil fuel industry, but as an evolving mechanism of governance that seeks to clarify and enforce corporate responsibility under climate law. Ripoll shifts the conversation from technological inevitability to political and ethical prioritisation, arguing that the remaining carbon budget should be allocated according to collective social needs rather than commercial interests. She asserts that multinational energy companies can no longer position climate change solely as a matter for governments. Instead, she contends that businesses whose activities substantially contribute to greenhouse gas emissions carry independent legal obligations that courts are increasingly willing to recognise.
articles.content:
Genie Godula is pleased to welcome Justine Ripoll, Campaign Manager for French NGO "Notre Affaire à Tous", and one of four NGOs who brought the climate case against Total Energy. She argues that climate litigation not as a symbolic confrontation with the fossil fuel industry, but as an evolving mechanism of governance that seeks to clarify and enforce corporate responsibility under climate law. Ripoll shifts the conversation from technological inevitability to political and ethical prioritisation, arguing that the remaining carbon budget should be allocated according to collective social needs rather than commercial interests. She asserts that multinational energy companies can no longer position climate change solely as a matter for governments. Instead, she contends that businesses whose activities substantially contribute to greenhouse gas emissions carry independent legal obligations that courts are increasingly willing to recognise.

Video by: Genie GODULA
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant legal and societal development where multinational polluting companies are being held accountable under French climate law. This represents a meaningful advancement in climate governance and corporate responsibility, with broad implications for environmental justice and climate action. The focus on legal obligations for corporations to reduce emissions and prioritize social needs over commercial interests is a substantial positive change with wide-reaching impact.
articles.personality_title: French courts hold polluting companies like Total responsible for climate change
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In France, there is growing attention on how big companies contribute to climate change and what rules they must follow. Some groups are using the law to make sure companies take responsibility for their impact on the environment.

**What happened** – Justine Ripoll, a campaign manager for the French NGO "Notre Affaire à Tous," is part of a team that took the energy company Total to court. They argue that companies like Total must follow climate laws and can’t just leave climate issues to governments. This legal case is one of several that show courts are starting to require companies to act responsibly about their greenhouse gas emissions.

**Impact** – This case is important because it changes how we think about responsibility for climate change. It shows that companies, not just governments, have legal duties to reduce pollution. It also pushes for using the Earth's limited carbon budget to meet social needs instead of focusing only on business profits. This can lead to fairer decisions about how we protect the planet.

**What’s next step** – Courts in France and other countries may continue to recognize the legal duties of companies to fight climate change. This could lead to stricter rules and more cases holding companies accountable. It also encourages businesses to rethink their impact and prioritize the planet and people over profits.

**One-sentence takeaway** – French courts are starting to require polluting companies like Total to follow climate laws and take responsibility for their role in global warming.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les tribunaux français tiennent Total et autres pollueurs responsables du changement climatique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En France, l’attention grandit sur la contribution des grandes entreprises au changement climatique et les règles qu’elles doivent respecter. Certains groupes utilisent la loi pour s’assurer que ces entreprises prennent leurs responsabilités envers l’environnement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Justine Ripoll, responsable de campagne de l’ONG française "Notre Affaire à Tous," fait partie d’une équipe qui a porté plainte contre la société énergétique Total. Ils affirment que des entreprises comme Total doivent respecter les lois climatiques et ne peuvent pas laisser la responsabilité uniquement aux gouvernements. Cette affaire est l’une des nombreuses qui montrent que les tribunaux demandent aux entreprises d’agir de façon responsable face aux émissions de gaz à effet de serre.

**Impact** – Cette affaire est importante car elle change notre façon de voir la responsabilité dans le changement climatique. Elle montre que les entreprises ont des devoirs légaux pour réduire la pollution, pas seulement les gouvernements. Elle pousse aussi à utiliser le budget carbone limité de la Terre pour répondre aux besoins sociaux plutôt qu’aux profits commerciaux, ce qui peut mener à des décisions plus justes pour protéger la planète.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les tribunaux en France et ailleurs pourraient continuer à reconnaître les devoirs légaux des entreprises face au changement climatique. Cela pourrait entraîner des règles plus strictes et plus de procès tenant les entreprises responsables. Cela encourage aussi les entreprises à repenser leur impact et à privilégier la planète et les personnes plutôt que les profits.

**Phrase clé** – Les tribunaux français exigent désormais que des entreprises polluantes comme Total respectent les lois climatiques et assument leur rôle dans le réchauffement global.
articles.personality_title_es: Los tribunales franceses responsabilizan a Total y otras empresas contaminantes por el cambio climático
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Francia, cada vez hay más atención sobre cómo las grandes empresas contribuyen al cambio climático y las reglas que deben seguir. Algunos grupos usan la ley para asegurar que las empresas asuman su responsabilidad con el medio ambiente.

**Qué pasó** – Justine Ripoll, directora de campaña de la ONG francesa "Notre Affaire à Tous," forma parte de un equipo que llevó a la empresa energética Total a los tribunales. Argumentan que empresas como Total deben cumplir con las leyes climáticas y no pueden dejar el problema solo en manos del gobierno. Este caso es uno de varios que muestran que los tribunales empiezan a exigir a las empresas actuar con responsabilidad respecto a las emisiones de gases de efecto invernadero.

**Impacto** – Este caso es importante porque cambia la forma en que pensamos sobre la responsabilidad del cambio climático. Muestra que las empresas, no solo los gobiernos, tienen deberes legales para reducir la contaminación. También impulsa a usar el limitado presupuesto de carbono de la Tierra para satisfacer necesidades sociales en lugar de solo intereses comerciales, lo que puede llevar a decisiones más justas para proteger el planeta.

**Próximos pasos** – Los tribunales en Francia y otros países pueden seguir reconociendo los deberes legales de las empresas para combatir el cambio climático. Esto podría traer reglas más estrictas y más casos que responsabilicen a las empresas. También anima a los negocios a repensar su impacto y priorizar el planeta y las personas sobre las ganancias.

**Frase clave** – Los tribunales franceses empiezan a exigir que empresas contaminantes como Total cumplan con las leyes climáticas y asuman su responsabilidad en el calentamiento global.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting depicting a large oil rig gently balanced on a scale opposite a group of diverse, interconnected hands made of light and earth tones, symbolizing collective social responsibility and legal accountability under climate law, set against a calm, natural background with soft, muted colors.

articles.title: LIVE: From conflict to stability, how the UN is supporting peacebuilding around the world
articles.summary: As wars persist in different parts of the world, it’s perhaps easy to lose sight of the many countries that have managed to recover from past violence, sustain peace and prevent conflict. The UN Peacebuilding Fund, which is marking its 20th year of operation in 2026, has contributed to many of these success stories. UN News app users click here.
articles.content: As wars persist in different parts of the world, it’s perhaps easy to lose sight of the many countries that have managed to recover from past violence, sustain peace and prevent conflict. The UN Peacebuilding Fund, which is marking its 20th year of operation in 2026, has contributed to many of these success stories. UN News app users click here.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the UN Peacebuilding Fund's significant contribution over 20 years to helping countries recover from conflict, sustain peace, and prevent further violence. This represents a meaningful, broad impact on global peace and stability, which is inspiring and beneficial to many societies worldwide.
articles.personality_title: How the UN Peacebuilding Fund Helps Countries Build Peace
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many countries around the world have experienced wars and conflicts. While some conflicts continue, others have managed to recover and keep peace.

**What happened** – The United Nations (UN) Peacebuilding Fund has been working for nearly 20 years to help countries recover from violence, maintain peace, and stop new conflicts from starting. It will celebrate its 20th anniversary in 2026.

**Impact** – The Fund has supported many countries in rebuilding their communities and governments after wars. This work helps people live safer lives and prevents violence from returning, showing that peace is possible even after conflict.

**What's next step** – As the Fund reaches its 20th year, the UN plans to continue supporting peace efforts in more countries, learning from past successes to improve how they help communities stay peaceful.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN Peacebuilding Fund has played a key role in helping countries recover from conflict and build lasting peace over the past two decades.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment le Fonds de consolidation de la paix de l’ONU aide les pays à construire la paix
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux pays dans le monde ont connu des guerres et des conflits. Alors que certains conflits continuent, d’autres ont réussi à se reconstruire et à maintenir la paix.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le Fonds de consolidation de la paix des Nations Unies (ONU) travaille depuis presque 20 ans pour aider les pays à se remettre de la violence, à garder la paix et à empêcher de nouveaux conflits.

**Impact** – Le Fonds a aidé de nombreux pays à reconstruire leurs communautés et leurs gouvernements après la guerre. Ce travail permet aux gens de vivre en sécurité et évite que la violence ne recommence, montrant que la paix est possible même après un conflit.

**Prochaine étape** – À l’occasion de son 20e anniversaire, l’ONU prévoit de continuer à soutenir les efforts de paix dans plus de pays, en tirant des leçons des succès passés pour mieux aider les communautés à rester pacifiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Fonds de consolidation de la paix de l’ONU a joué un rôle important pour aider les pays à se remettre des conflits et à construire une paix durable depuis vingt ans.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo el Fondo para la Consolidación de la Paz de la ONU ayuda a los países a construir la paz
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos países en el mundo han vivido guerras y conflictos. Mientras algunos conflictos continúan, otros han logrado recuperarse y mantener la paz.

**Qué pasó** – El Fondo para la Consolidación de la Paz de las Naciones Unidas (ONU) ha trabajado casi 20 años para ayudar a los países a recuperarse de la violencia, mantener la paz y evitar que surjan nuevos conflictos.

**Impacto** – El Fondo ha apoyado a muchos países a reconstruir sus comunidades y gobiernos después de la guerra. Este trabajo ayuda a que las personas vivan más seguras y evita que la violencia regrese, mostrando que la paz es posible incluso después de un conflicto.

**Próximo paso** – Al cumplir 20 años, la ONU planea seguir apoyando esfuerzos de paz en más países, aprendiendo de los éxitos anteriores para mejorar su ayuda a las comunidades.

**Resumen en una oración** – El Fondo para la Consolidación de la Paz de la ONU ha sido clave para ayudar a países a recuperarse de conflictos y construir una paz duradera durante las últimas dos décadas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of intertwined olive branches forming a sturdy bridge over a calm river flowing between two contrasting landscapes—one side showing faded ruins and the other flourishing green fields—bathed in warm, soft natural tones symbolizing lasting peace and recovery fostered by global unity.
